title: Some sequence of multiple GPGME mechanisms / signing + verification operations causes hangs
body:
See the `FIXME FIXME` comment in tests added by https://github.com/containers/image/pull/1776 .

- GPGME_DEBUG=9 shows that a `gpgme_op_verify` hangs permanently polling on an already closed file descriptor
- That polling never terminates apparently because a `_gpgme_io_close` of that file descriptor does not call a close handler.
- It’s not clear why that close handler is not called, or not found.
- Curiously, a previous `gpgme_op_sign` operation supplying a passphrase (which succeeds just fine!) leaves around an entry in GPGME’s `notify_table` for that file descriptor. That doesn’t quite explain things (it might cause the _wrong_ handler to be called, but there is no log to that effect either).

I wasn’t able to track this down further within a few hours: the GPGME file descriptor tracking / state handling code is very newbie-unfriendly. It might go much better with full debug info…

comments.body: This bug has been opened for 2 1/2 years.   I suspect that I may be experiencing this issue now (https://github.com/cri-o/cri-o/issues/8906), and often.  It this still being pursued?

I don’t know that anyone is actively working on this.

The sigstore signing format, with an entirely unrelated implementation, is what I would generally recommend for new deployments; and there is containers/image#2569 proposing to move away from gpgme (only with an opt-in? TBD). So this signing mechanism is not a primary upstream focus, and so far indications seem to _suggest_ it’s not a bug in this repo anyway. (I realize none of this helps current users of that mechanism.)

@mtrmac 

Thank you.  I had wondered if OpenPGP had been considered as an alternative to gpgme,  and the issue that you linked seems to confirm that it has.  

I don't know that sigstore signing is an option for us at this time, but something to look into.    

I just wish there was a way to allow the user to have the option to disable the signature verification on container create.  

Thank you for your input.
Disabling signature verification is “simple”: edit the policy.
… if you are specifically talking about signature verification at CRI-O container create vs. CRI-O image pull, the container-create one is necessary to properly enforce per-namespace policies.

@mtrmac 

In our environment,  we 'pre-pull' the images during our first boot process (with buildah)   Then after this, we were setting the crio policy to not allow pulling any additional images.  In that way, we have control of what images we have, and not allow users to pull additional images (This is a control-plane appliance where we want control of what runs in it).  

Starting with 1.31,  that no longer seemed to be an option, and that we had to have the signature validation policy, or allow images to to pulled with no validation.    Unless I am missing something basic here (and that is entirely possible)

I understand the need, and  why the container create check was needed.   I am just not convinced that in our environment, it is really needed to validate on the create (I would have to think about that).  

Of course, if all was working,  it would not be in issue.
